---
title: "Client Report - Show me!"
subtitle: "Unit 4 Task 3"
author: "Rebecca Hockman"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import polars as pl
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.inspection import permutation_importance
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score, classification_report
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn import metrics
import requests
import io
# add the additional libraries you need to import for ML here

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```


```{python}
# import your data here using polars and the URL
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/byuidatascience/data4dwellings/master/data-raw/dwellings_ml/dwellings_ml.csv"

response = requests.get(url)

df = pl.read_csv(io.BytesIO(response.content))


# the OG
y = df.select("before1980")
X = df.drop(["before1980", "yrbuilt", "parcel"])
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
X_np = X.to_numpy()
y_np = y.to_numpy().ravel()
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X_np,
    y_np,
    test_size=0.2,
    random_state=42
)
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
model = RandomForestClassifier(
    n_estimators=200,
    random_state=42
)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score
y_pred = model.predict(X_test)

```

## QUESTION 1

1. __Repeat the classification exercise using 2 different algorithms/models. Explain the differences between the 3 models__ (your original one, and the 2 new ones for this task).

The first new model I used is the decision tree. This is similar to the random forest but it is one tree rather than many, which is easy to interperet but over fits the data, so it can be less reliable.

The second new model I used is the logistic regression model. This gave me two different accuracies. The decision tree accuracy, which is the actual accuracy score, and the logistic regression accuracy which shows the accuracy of the linear probability of the data relationship. The logistical regression shows the linear relationships between the features and the year.

```{python}
# Include and execute your code here


dt_model = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42)
dt_model.fit(X_train, y_train)

y_pred_dt = dt_model.predict(X_test)
accuracy_dt = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_dt)
print("Decision Tree Accuracy:", accuracy_dt)

log_model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
log_model.fit(X_train, y_train)

y_pred_log = log_model.predict(X_test)
accuracy_log = accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_log)
print("Logistic Regression Accuracy:", accuracy_log)

```


## QUESTION 2

1. For each model (your original one, and the 2 new ones for this task), display the classification report and the permutation importances for the most important features.  Discuss which model you would recommend to the Client and why.

I would recomend the Random Forest Classifier to a client because it combines many different decision trees to show the relationships between the different factors to the goal. In this case, it helps us identify the probability of a house being built before 1980 based on the different qualifiers.

This has the best accuracy because it runs the several different possibilities and gives us the most likely statistics.

```{python}
# Include and execute your code here
# The OG
rf_model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200, random_state=42)
rf_model.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred_rf = rf_model.predict(X_test)
print("=== Random Forest Classification Report ===")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_rf))

# Decision Tree
dt_model = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42)
dt_model.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred_dt = dt_model.predict(X_test)
print("=== Decision Tree Classification Report ===")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_dt))

# Logistic Regression
log_model = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
log_model.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred_log = log_model.predict(X_test)
print("=== Logistic Regression Classification Report ===")
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_log))

```

```{python}

def perm_importance_table(model, X_test, y_test, feature_names, top_n=5):
    results = permutation_importance(model, X_test, y_test, n_repeats=10, random_state=17)
    df_imp = pl.DataFrame({
        "feature": feature_names,
        "perm_imp": results.importances_mean
    }).sort("perm_imp", descending=True)
    return df_imp.head(top_n)
```


```{python}
# The OG
rf_top = perm_importance_table(rf_model, X_test, y_test, X.columns)
print("Random Forest Top Features:")
print(rf_top)
```


```{python}
# Decision Tree 
dt_top = perm_importance_table(dt_model, X_test, y_test, X.columns)
print("Decision Tree Top Features:")
print(dt_top)
```


```{python}
# Logistic Regression 
log_top = perm_importance_table(log_model, X_test, y_test, X.columns)
print("Logistic Regression Top Features:")
print(log_top)
```
